scenario_name: Scenario 14 — Anti-Pattern Detection in Existing Architecture
justification: All 5 anti-patterns identified by name (shared DB, synchronous notification fan-in, god endpoint, REST-as-jobs, direct cross-service DB reads). Remediation is clearly prioritized into 5 sequenced phases — not treated as equally urgent. Shared database identified as CRITICAL and placed in Phase 2. REST-based jobs explicitly called fragile (timeout risk, no retry/backoff, no idempotency). Detailed trade-offs table for fixing shared database (lose cross-service joins, eventual consistency, rejected alternatives). Incremental Strangler Fig approach explicitly recommended throughout — never big-bang rewrite. Minor deviation: direct DB reads (Reporting) classified as MEDIUM severity and placed last (Phase 5) rather than as highest risk alongside shared DB, but MJ provides defensible reasoning (read-only access is less dangerous than read-write sharing) and still identifies it as a clear anti-pattern requiring remediation.

observations.text: All 5 anti-patterns identified with detailed 'Why it hurts' explanations that go beyond surface-level naming
Remediation plan is rigorously sequenced with explicit 'Why first/second/third' rationale for each phase
Strangler Fig pattern recommended throughout — explicitly states 'Never rewrite'
Trade-offs table includes gains, sacrifices, and rejected alternatives for each decision
Identifies outbox pattern as non-optional complementary pattern — shows depth of distributed systems knowledge
Includes practical pitfalls section warning against common migration mistakes
Direct cross-service DB reads (Reporting) classified as MEDIUM and placed in Phase 5 (last), while rubric expects it grouped with shared DB as highest risk
Notification fan-in prioritized as Phase 1 over shared DB with strong reasoning (highest blast radius, easiest fix) — shows pragmatic sequencing by effort-to-risk-reduction ratio
Cultural concern raised about anti-pattern recurrence — demonstrates architectural maturity beyond just technical fixes

agent_output_excerpt:
## PR Review — MJ (Architecture)

### Executive Summary

This system exhibits five distinct architectural anti-patterns that have compounded over 3 years of organic growth. The most critical issues are the shared database between Order and Courier services (destroying service autonomy) and the synchronous notification fan-in (creating a system-wide availability bottleneck). Left unaddressed, these patterns will increasingly constrain team velocity, degrade reliability, and make the system progre
